prompt:
Frontend-infra ticket. Our web client applies mutations optimistically — the UI updates immediately, the server confirms or declines later — and every feature team hand-rolls the bookkeeping with scattered setState calls; we ship a rollback bug roughly every sprint. I want the whole mechanism in one dependency-free TypeScript module: optimistic.ts. It's a synchronous state machine — the transport layer stays outside and calls confirm/reject when responses come back, so no promises, no timers.

Model: the store wraps a record of key → value (values are JSON-ish: primitives, arrays, plain objects). Local mutations enter a pending FIFO queue; confirmed state only changes when the server answers.

export class OptimisticStore:
- constructor(base = {}, opts?: { policy?: Policy; onConflict?: (local, server, op) => void }). Copy base at construction — the caller mutating their object afterwards must not leak into the store. Default policy is serverWins.
- apply(key, value): string — queue a pending set, return its op id. Ids are 'op-1', 'op-2', ... in creation order per store instance. Coalescing: when the queue's TAIL op has the same key, do NOT create a new op — overwrite the tail's value and return the tail's EXISTING id (the id counter doesn't advance). Only the tail coalesces: apply to k, then j, then k again is three distinct ops, because op order is sacred.
- state(): the confirmed state with every pending op layered over it in queue order. A fresh object on every call — callers mutate what you hand them and that must never touch the store.
- confirmed(): just the confirmed state, same fresh-object rule.
- pending(): the queue as [{ id, key, value }, ...] — copies, in order.
- confirm(opId, serverValue): the server accepted the head op and reports the value it actually stored. Resolution is strictly FIFO: if opId isn't the queue head, throw Error('op "<opId>" is not at the head of the queue'); if it's not in the queue at all, throw Error('unknown op "<opId>"') — the unknown-id check wins when both would apply. If serverValue deep-equals the op's local value (structural equality, nested arrays/objects included), it's a clean commit: confirmed[key] = local value, and onConflict is NOT called. Otherwise it's a conflict: call onConflict(localValue, serverValue, op) if configured, then confirmed[key] = policy.resolve(localValue, serverValue, op).
- reject(opId, serverValue?): the server declined the head op (same head-only and unknown-id errors). The local change rolls back — the op leaves the queue and the policy is NOT consulted: even a clientWins store loses a rejected op, rejection is authoritative. If serverValue was passed (anything !== undefined) the server also told us the real value: confirmed[key] = serverValue; omitted → confirmed stays untouched. onConflict ALWAYS fires on reject, with (localValue, serverValue-or-undefined, op).

Pending ops on the same key later in the queue keep overlaying state() after an earlier op on that key is rejected — recompute, don't cache.

export interface Policy { name: string; resolve(local, server, op): unknown }
export const serverWins: Policy — name 'server-wins', resolve returns the server value.
export const clientWins: Policy — name 'client-wins', resolve returns the local value.
Custom policy objects must work: whatever resolve returns is what gets committed.

The op object handed to onConflict and resolve carries { id, key, value } where value is the local value at resolution time (i.e. after any coalescing).

Contract: node --test test_optimistic.ts (node 26 runs .ts via type stripping, so plain typed TS — no enums, no namespaces). Don't modify the test file.
